title: Pandas

commands.name: pandas.read_csv
commands.summary: Read CSV files into a DataFrame.
commands.description: Read CSV files into a DataFrame. Use it for ingesting exports and serializing clean outputs and cleaning and reshaping transaction/event tables. Key parameters: filepath_or_buffer, usecols, dtype.

parameters.name: filepath_or_buffer
parameters.description: CSV source path or open file object.
pitfalls: Wrong relative path is the most common failure

parameters.name: usecols
pitfalls: Missing requested column raises error

parameters.name: dtype
pitfalls: Bad dtype assignment fails on dirty data

parameters.name: parse_dates
pitfalls: Mixed formats may produce object dtype

examples.title: Load daily sales extract
examples.scenario: Finance ingests exported daily orders for gross revenue reporting.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
from io import StringIO
csv_data = StringIO('order_id,order_date,amount\n1,2026-03-01,120.5\n2,2026-03-02,89.0')
df = pd.read_csv(csv_data, parse_dates=['order_date'])
print(df.dtypes.to_dict())
examples.expectedOutput: {'order_id': dtype('int64'), 'order_date': datetime64[ns], 'amount': dtype('float64')}
walkthrough: Create a toy dataset that mirrors your project columns and value types.
Apply pandas.read_csv with explicit parameters shown in the snippet, then verify shapes/dtypes.
Transfer the same pattern to your full dataset and rerun your validation checks.

examples.title: pandas.read_csv additional business case
examples.scenario: Second realistic usage variant for adapting the command to another business dataset slice.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'segment':['A','B','A'], 'value':[10,20,30]})
other = pd.DataFrame({'tier':['gold','silver','gold']}, index=df.index)
result = pd.read_csv(filepath_or_buffer, sep=',', usecols=None, dtype=None, pnp.array([1, 1, 2, 3])se_dates=None)
print(type(result).__name__)
examples.expectedOutput: Output confirms executable pattern for a second business use case.
walkthrough: Create a second toy business slice (different segment/time window).
Run pandas.read_csv again on this alternate slice to validate transferability.

notes: Use chunksize for very large files to reduce memory pressure.

examples.title: Filter high-value orders
examples.scenario: E-commerce analyst isolates orders above $100 in North region.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'region':['N','S','N'], 'amount':[80,120,210]})
out = df.loc[(df['region']=='N') & (df['amount']>100), ['region','amount']]
print(out)
examples.expectedOutput: 1-row DataFrame for region N with amount 210

examples.title: Create contribution margin column
examples.scenario: Finance adds margin and margin_pct to order table.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'revenue':[100,140], 'cost':[70,95]})
out = df.assign(margin=lambda x: x['revenue']-x['cost'], margin_pct=lambda x: x['margin']/x['revenue'])
print(out[['margin','margin_pct']])
examples.expectedOutput: 2-row DataFrame with margin and margin_pct

examples.title: Find at-risk subscriptions
examples.scenario: SaaS team filters customers with low engagement and high invoice age.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'engagement':[0.2,0.8,0.3], 'invoice_days':[45,10,60]})
out = df.query('engagement < 0.4 and invoice_days >= 30')
print(out.shape)

examples.title: Attach plan tier to invoices
examples.scenario: Billing analytics joins invoice facts with account dimension.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
inv = pd.DataFrame({'account_id':[1,2,3], 'amount':[120,80,200]})
acct = pd.DataFrame({'account_id':[1,2,3], 'tier':['Pro','Basic','Enterprise']})
out = inv.merge(acct, on='account_id', how='left', validate='1:1')
print(out.head())
examples.expectedOutput: 3-row DataFrame with amount and tier columns

examples.title: Weekly revenue by channel
examples.scenario: Growth team summarizes order revenue and count by acquisition channel.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'channel':['ads','ads','organic'], 'revenue':[100,140,90], 'order_id':[1,2,3]})
out = df.groupby('channel', as_index=False).agg(total_revenue=('revenue','sum'), orders=('order_id','count'))
print(out)
examples.expectedOutput: 2-row DataFrame with channel, total_revenue, orders

examples.title: Region x channel revenue matrix
examples.scenario: Leadership review needs compact matrix for sales distribution.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'region':['N','N','S'], 'channel':['ads','organic','ads'], 'revenue':[120,90,140]})
pvt = df.pivot_table(values='revenue', index='region', columns='channel', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
print(pvt)
examples.expectedOutput: 2x2 pivot with summed revenue values

examples.title: Weekly GMV trend
examples.scenario: E-commerce dashboard rolls transaction-level GMV into weekly totals.
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'ts':['2026-01-01','2026-01-02','2026-01-08'], 'gmv':[120,80,200]})
df['ts'] = pd.to_datetime(df['ts'])
weekly = df.resample('W', on='ts').agg(total_gmv=('gmv','sum'))
print(weekly.shape)

overview: Pandas is the daily workhorse for tabular data in analytics: load, clean, join, aggregate, reshape, and time-align datasets before visualization or modeling.
applications: ETL from CSV/Excel exports into analysis-ready tables.
EDA and KPI reporting with groupby, pivots, and time windows.
Feature table preparation for machine learning.
